author: @ogi22
text:
There was one thing nagging me since you show that glass bearing and i couldn't put my finger on it till now.
No lubrication. And if you think of a wind turbine, that thing will spin fast. Fast spinning bearings, no lubrication = heat. A lot of it.
So, my little idea is: using just a bit of that wind power, add something like afuel pump from old cars (mechanically coupled with that gear). Water after all is a lubricant, so one solution would be to pour water over it in a closed cycle. And since it's 3D printed i could see there also some PTFE or silicone lube/cooling system.
Anyways, that's my two cents idea to this nice setup.
Have a good day!😊
[edit]
Just took a peek in the comments and it looks like i'm not the only one who pointed this out 😁
You have built yourself a commuity of really nice tinkerers there!

author: @SiwyWabbit
text:
I have built wind turbines for over 55 years. I use a MUCH SIMPLER method, namely, nylon bushings.  Nylon is uber-machine able, cheap and weatherproof. I use a Nylon worm gear on the main shaft driving a helically-cut spur gear (down gear) that has a crank pin attached to an air pump.  My windmills all compress air.  I turn compressed air into useable mechanical energy through both piston as well as overshot turbines.  I subscribe to the "KISS" Theory.
Keep It Simple, Sir.

author: @geraldconklin655
text: Just a tip. Ball bearings should ALWAYS be an odd number for smoothest/longest lived service. This I learned from a master bicycle tech and after building many, many bicycle wheels.

replies.author: @MicahMelnyk
replies.text: why is this the case?

replies.author: @geraldconklin655
replies.text: @MicahMelnyk  I still don't know why, but it's true. I was told by the master mechanic, and I tested it many times. It was always better/smoother with an odd number of bearings.

author: @colrodrick8784
text:
I have said it before. You have done all the thinking and puzzling, what offing for us.

I can just put my brain on a shelf and go to sleep.  Or better than that, have a nice cuppa to warm up to.  Freezing this morning.

I’m sure that I’m not alone, wondering where all this tinkering is going to end up.

I reckon that if we get a little quiet device that can turn wind into an amp at 12volts on average, what a breakthrough.  24 ah a day.  Enough to power our fridge/freezer on the road.  Ok it draws around 4 amps, but cycles on and off, so say it’s off half the time.

author: @user-hf3ym7lh4d
text: Note that plastic isn't nearly as thermally conductive as metal, so the heat from internal friction will build up much more before escaping. Don't be surprised if the bearings soften and deform under sustained load.

text: great for prototyping. You could 3d print the gears then use (high quality!) clay to make molds and cast in a corrosion resistant metal. I may give this a go with an ingot that is sitting in my workshop from a bronze age replica sword project a couple of years ago...

author: @ffaubert1
text:
Excellent video Robert!

One caveat for all you 3-D print folks, PLA is not UV resistant. PLA will fairly quickly get brittle, crack and warp when exposed to sunlight. This is fine for something like a plastic garden gnome but not good for anything that will have stresses like handles and gears 

ASA (not ABS) is much more UV resistant and only slightly more expensive than PLA but is more finicky to print than PLA.

Just a heads-up.

replies.author: @corydwagner
replies.text: I was also going to mention this. Although you could paint it with UV-resistant paint. I'd be more concerned about how the PLA would deal with heat and physical stress.

replies.author: @hissst69
replies.text: I would just keep it shaded.. but Robert is just prototyping I'm sure he would use the plastic best suited for whatever specific applicationit required.

replies.author: @ronaldheath453
replies.text: I use PETG for things that are outdoors and in partial sun. It changes color over time, but is fairly rugged. Requires higher print temperatures, slower speed and I need to print it with blue tape on a heated bed.

replies.author: @ffaubert1
replies.text: @hissst69 I agree. I was mentioning it more for those of us beginners who are trying their hand at making 3-D equipment that will be used outside.

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Nice video again!  However, you should mention  and add lubrication  (grease) to really make the gears turn easily.  Also, you say that the gears will not rust.  However, outdoors all bearing will be subjected to dust, pollen, and small air-born particles that will contaminate the bearing surfaces and impede their turning over time.  That is why, commercial outdoor bearings have race-caps to help keep them cleaner longer.  Snap-on caps with a hole for the shaft would be a nice addition to finish off your wind generator!

replies.author: @davidellis1355
replies.text: Any particular greese ?

replies.author: @JaenFoster
replies.text: Grease will actually make the problem of dirt and dust worse, as it'll stick to the grease and won't be able to work its way back out, eventually forming an abrasive paste. A dry lubricant like graphite or moly is best for dry and dusty environments. Grease and (light) oil is best used in damp or humid environments.

Ever thought about using a (broken) rear-axle, with differential, of a small vehicle?
These are already weather and dust proof.
And since you only need the connection of one wheel (not both) to the driveshaft, you can probably get one very cheap at a scrapyard.
Just an idea...
